ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a Brazil nut entry (FOODON_03301710); the roasted and salted preparation variant is not separately coded — closest_parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LN covers Brazil nuts as a commodity; roasted and salted preparation is not separately coded in FoodEx2 — closest_parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229493001 covers Brazil nut as a substance; roasted salted preparation variant not separately coded.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of roasted salted Brazil nuts is approximately 30 g (about 6 whole nuts), providing around 201 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Roasted Brazil Nut, Salted — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F09030039 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, and polyunsaturated fat are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 12078 (Nuts, brazilnuts, dried, unblanched) as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions; values adjusted proportionally for roasted preparation per USDA FoodData Central entry 168594 (Nuts, brazilnuts, dried, unblanched — roasted analogue). Selenium value sourced from USDA FoodData Central.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for roasted salted Brazil nuts in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 25 is estimated by analogy with mixed nuts and other tree nuts (GI range 15–30) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Brazil nuts have very low carbohydrate content (9.6 g/100g) and high fat and protein, consistent with a low GI. Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (25 × 9.6 × 0.30) / 100 = 0.72 × 30 / 100 ≈ 2.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High potassium-to-sodium ratio (620 mg K : 78 mg Na per 100 g) consistent with DASH diet principles; calcium 200 mg/100 g supports vasodilation
hypertension.guidance: Roasted salted Brazil nuts are suitable for people with hypertension in strict portions of 30 g (approx. 6 nuts) per day, as the high potassium and calcium content supports blood pressure regulation, but the very high selenium content (>1900 mcg/100 g) imposes a hard upper limit of 2–3 nuts daily regardless of blood pressure goals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (9.6 g/100 g), low sugar (3.1 g/100 g), high protein (14.9 g/100 g) and high unsaturated fat content yield a very low estimated GI (~25) and minimal glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Brazil nuts are appropriate for Type 2 diabetes management in portions of 30 g per day due to their very low glycaemic index and insulin-sensitising unsaturated fat profile, but the selenium toxicity ceiling of 2–3 nuts per day must be observed as a hard limit independent of glycaemic goals.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (69.1 g/100 g) with saturated fat ~15.1 g/100 g exceeds the 12 g/100 g threshold; however, MUFA + PUFA (~45 g/100 g) dominate and selenium is a potent antioxidant that reduces oxidative LDL damage
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Brazil nuts may be included in a heart-healthy diet at a strictly controlled portion of 30 g per day (approx. 6 nuts), as the dominant unsaturated fat profile and high selenium antioxidant activity are cardioprotective, but the elevated saturated fat content (15.1 g/100 g) and very high caloric density (669 kcal/100 g) require careful portion control to avoid adverse lipid effects.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Richest dietary selenium source (antioxidant, reduces inflammatory cytokines); MUFA + PUFA dominant; saturated fat 15.1 g/100 g exceeds MUFA/PUFA carve-out ceiling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Brazil nuts provide clinically meaningful selenium and unsaturated fat for RA inflammation management at 30 g per day, but saturated fat exceeding 15 g/100 g and the selenium toxicity ceiling of 2–3 nuts daily require strict portion discipline.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted salted Brazil nuts (Bertholletia excelsa) are large, cream-coloured tree nuts native to the Amazon basin of South America, commercially sourced primarily from Brazil, Bolivia, and Peru. They are dry-roasted to develop a rich, buttery flavour and seasoned with salt, producing a crisp, crunchy texture with a distinctive savoury-nutty taste. Nutritionally, they are one of the most energy-dense foods available at 669 kcal per 100 g, with a fat content of 69.1 g/100 g dominated by monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fatty acids (~45 g/100 g combined) alongside saturated fat of approximately 15.1 g/100 g. Protein is substantial at 14.9 g/100 g. Carbohydrate is low at 9.6 g/100 g with only 3.1 g/100 g sugar, making the estimated glycaemic index very low (~25). Brazil nuts are the single richest dietary source of selenium, providing approximately 1917 mcg per 100 g — far exceeding the adult tolerable upper intake level of 400 mcg/day — which means the safe daily portion is strictly limited to 2–3 whole nuts (approximately 30 g). Potassium is very high at 620 mg/100 g and calcium at 200 mg/100 g, supporting cardiovascular and bone health. Clinically, Brazil nuts are suitable for Type 2 diabetes and hypertension management in controlled portions due to their low glycaemic index and favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio. For cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis, the dominant unsaturated fat and selenium antioxidant profile are beneficial, but the elevated saturated fat and extreme selenium density require strict portion limits. They are naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal.
common_user_queries: Are Brazil nuts good for diabetes?
How many Brazil nuts can I eat per day?
Are roasted salted Brazil nuts healthy?
Are Brazil nuts good for blood pressure?
Can I eat Brazil nuts if I have high cholesterol?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F09030039, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Fatty acid fractions supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (entry 168594 / 12078) as an approved secondary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed journal, clearly documented. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (15.1 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (24.5 g/100 g), and polyunsaturated fat (20.6 g/100 g) are derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Brazil nuts (dried, unblanched) as HPB Foodo does not separately report fatty acid fractions; values are consistent with the total fat of 69.1 g/100 g reported by HPB. 2. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g/100 g — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for Brazil nuts. 3. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 g/100 g — naturally zero as Brazil nuts are a plant food containing no vitamin B12. 4. Fields set to -1 (data not available): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and moisture data for the roasted salted preparation variant are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database for this record; USDA FoodData Central values exist for dried unblanched Brazil nuts but were not mapped to avoid mixing preparation variants for these secondary fields. 5. Selenium_mcg of 1917.0 mcg/100 g is sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (entry 12078); this exceptionally high value is well-established in the literature and is the primary clinical safety concern for Brazil nut consumption. 6. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for roasted salted Brazil nuts exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 7. Rheumatoid arthritis health context is retained from the input record as an additional condition. 8. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; no mandatory local-language names apply under G6 multilingual rules — local_names fields are correctly null.
